statement: BlueprintProvider derives a normalized BlueprintSnapshot and BlueprintDelta for a requested commit and reports capability flags; GRAPHREFLY_STACK_BLUEPRINT_PROVIDER selects the implementation, and only graphrefly is supported in the MVP.

Each indexed commit in the flagship stack resolves to a deterministic BlueprintSnapshot hash; selecting a commit compares its snapshot with its semantic stack parent and distinguishes structural delta from affected-but-structurally-unchanged claim impact. Merge-parent selection is deferred outside the linear flagship stack.

A SemanticChangeRecord binds a commit or work unit to intent, blueprint claims, allowed source scopes, dependencies, required checks, and freshness witnesses. GPT-5.6 or a human may propose it, but it is not evidence until deterministic validation accepts and binds it.

Evidence is read-only reproducible input or output such as hashes, deltas, validated claims, approvals, and check results. GateResult is the read-only output of a deterministic function over current repository state, witnesses, contracts, policy, and evidence; local CLI and future CI runners execute the same function and cannot hand-edit its verdict.

The primary UI is a real Git commit and branch graph. Commit selection synchronizes a focused Blueprint structural delta, claim-impact context, Semantic Change Card, GateResult, reason codes, checks, reviewer actions, and an optional raw-diff drilldown.

The live path uses Codex with GRAPHREFLY_STACK_MODEL and GRAPHREFLY_STACK_REASONING_EFFORT overrides, defaults to gpt-5.6-sol and high, and records the exact effective model, effort, prompt version, and provider response provenance without allowing model output to set GateResult.

The v1 wire family is strict JSON Schema draft-07 under contracts/v1/schemas with URN schema IDs, required properties, additionalProperties false, no coercion or defaults, and pinned Ajv 8.20.0 validation for Task, GitStack, BlueprintProviderCapabilities, BlueprintSnapshot, BlueprintDelta, ChangePlan and WorkUnit, SemanticChangeRecord, CheckResult, GateResult, SelectiveReplan, BundleManifest, ReviewDecision, and golden cases.

GraphReFly topology hashes use upstream canonicalTopologyBytes with SHA-256. Stack artifact hashes use RFC 8785 JCS, UTF-8, and SHA-256; inputs must satisfy I-JSON, manifest and README are excluded from self-referential artifact hashes, and gate input digests exclude GateResult, model explanation, ReviewDecision, wall-clock metadata, and randomness.

A stable Git patch ID is only a rebase candidate key. Acceptance proves the current Git object, whitespace-sensitive canonical diff digest, actual changed paths, semantic parent, and required checks. Records are immutable; a successful unaffected rebase creates a new deterministic SemanticChangeRecord with rebindFrom rather than mutating or blindly trusting the prior record.

Each accepted SemanticChangeRecord binds commit OID, stable patch ID, canonical diff digest, semantic parent record and commit, blueprint hash, policy revision, allowed source-scope digest, required-check IDs, contract version, and provider ID and version. A blueprint hash change triggers claim-impact revalidation; it invalidates only impacted claims and may deterministically rebind unaffected work.

Gate validation order is schema, provider capability, Git binding, source scope, semantic parent and dependencies, blueprint and policy freshness, required checks, then artifact integrity. Verdict is pass, blocked, or error; work units follow ChangePlan order and reasons follow validation stage then code order. The v1 reason-code set is schema invalid, provider capability unsupported, blueprint diagnostics error, commit not found, commit binding mismatch, patch ID ambiguous, source scope violation, semantic parent stale, dependency invalid, blueprint witness stale, policy revision stale, session write requires broker, required check missing, required check failed, and artifact hash mismatch.

The GraphReFly v1 provider reports commit snapshots, canonical topology bytes, node metadata, subgraphs, diagnostics, and structural delta as supported; merge-parent selection is unsupported, and claim-impact evaluation belongs to Stack core rather than the provider.

ReviewDecision is separately attributed human evidence and never edits or supplies GateResult. The semantic v1 artifact remains approve, defer, or reject evidence bound to SemanticChangeRecord IDs; a generic repository review uses its own strict decision artifact bound to the exact parent commit, selected commit, Blueprint hash, base and head range. The authless local product labels a reviewer without claiming verified identity, and the UI presents deterministic facts, any GateResult, and the human decision as distinct states.

STACK-3 locks byte-stable current-valid, clean-rebase-semantic-stale, unrelated-change-still-valid, stale-parent, forged-or-wrong-scope, and fresh-selective-replan cases. Every case validates against v1 schemas, canonicalizes repeatably, matches a checked-in SHA-256 digest, and fixes GateResult verdict and reason ordering without implementing the STACK-5 gate engine.

The private pnpm workspace has four TypeScript boundaries: @graphrefly-stack/contracts owns strict v1 validation, JCS and artifact digests; @graphrefly-stack/core owns deterministic gate, freshness, impact and replan behavior plus ports; @graphrefly-stack/cli is the only composition root and owns the executable, system adapters and local HTTP server; @graphrefly-stack/review is a React 19 and Vite 8 projection that consumes versioned data and never owns verdict semantics. Production review runs in the CLI process without a database or services split.

The canonical graphrefly-stack surface exposes fixture create, plan, gate, replan, review and export. Every non-review command supports --json and emits exactly one versioned envelope to stdout while diagnostics use stderr; execution success exits 0, a successfully computed blocked GateResult exits 2, and usage, schema or runtime failure exits 1. The review command binds to 127.0.0.1 by default and serves the built web projection from the same local process.

The CLI composition root invokes system Git with shell disabled and allowlisted argv, confines fixture mutations to an explicit output repository, and derives GraphReFly snapshots for the flagship through a fixed entrypoint in a detached worktree using a scrubbed environment, read-only Node permission allowlists and a timeout. The core defines GitAdapter, BlueprintProvider and PlanProvider ports; only system Git, graphrefly and replay or server-side Codex plan providers are implemented in the MVP, and browser code never receives credentials.

Source templates and reviewed redacted replay inputs live under fixtures; generated Git repositories, detached worktrees and raw provider responses live only under ignored .private paths; only redacted hash-verified bundles enter evidence/runs. Replay is the default plan mode and requires no credentials. Live must be selected explicitly, validates against the same contracts, records exact provenance, and cannot silently become replay; an explicitly enabled provider-failure fallback is labelled replay-fallback with the failure reason and replay artifact identity.

The server-side Codex TypeScript SDK runs plan and selective-replan turns read-only with approval never, network tools disabled, an explicit model and reasoning effort, and per-turn structured output schemas. Stack post-validates the strict proposal contract, preserves locked work-unit IDs, dependencies, scopes, claim IDs, checks and selective invalidation boundaries, then deterministically binds proposals without allowing model output to create or edit GateResult. Raw responses remain under .private; redacted live-run artifacts record SDK and CLI runtime versions, prompt version, thread, usage, response and output digests, and enter an explicitly selected bundle. Provider failure exits as live error unless replay fallback was explicitly enabled and labelled.

The flagship GraphReFly entrypoint imports the pinned upstream runtime, builds the repository's actual graph modules, and returns graph.blueprint({diagnostics:true}) output with caller provenance and upstream hashing plus describeToMermaid(graph.describe()) readable diagram source; Stack must reject provider payloads that are not GraphBlueprint v1, whose topology hash does not match upstream canonicalTopologyBytes, or whose diagram source is not the GraphReFly render projection of the same runtime graph. Review data includes A1 and per-work-unit commit snapshots plus deterministic parent deltas derived from those snapshots. Commit selection renders the GraphReFly-generated diagram with added and removed structure distinguished, and parses the canonical Git patch into file, hunk, old-line, and new-line rows for a GitHub-style viewer without treating raw patch text as the user interface.

The first production stack contract orders WorkUnits linearly for discovery, evaluation, and review, but a WorkUnit ID is a stable logical identity rather than an index or commit OID. Each accepted SemanticChangeRecord binds that identity to the current immutable Git object and explicit semantic parent; rebases create new bindings, and no v1 field may require encoding a WorkUnit's linear position into its identity.

A hosted control-plane envelope contains only schema-validated redacted evidence explicitly selected by repository policy, with content digests, redaction policy revision, runner provenance, and artifact allowlists. Raw source, unredacted GraphBlueprints, raw model or provider output, credentials, and repository execution remain local by default; broader uploads require an explicit scoped authorization recorded as evidence.

Before production repository generalization, a pinned published @graphrefly/ts release based on 0.2.1 or later must expose version-aware GraphBlueprint parsing and validation, verification of canonical topology hashes, deterministic readable rendering whose input is the GraphBlueprint itself, and canonical structural delta semantics including nodes, edges, metadata, and subgraphs. Stack validates the advertised upstream capabilities and retains ownership only of commit snapshots, Git bindings, claim impact, policy, evidence, and GateResult.

A repository is supported by the first usable product when it is a local Git worktree, pins a supported @graphrefly/ts release, exposes a repository-configured executable Blueprint entrypoint that returns the upstream GraphBlueprint contract without credentials or writes, and supplies resolvable linear base and head revisions. Stack resolves the target repository's pinned runtime, executes each revision in an isolated detached worktree, and fails closed on missing entrypoints, unsupported Blueprint capabilities, diagnostics errors, non-linear ancestry, or hash and renderer verification failure.

For a compatible repository and linear base-to-head range, Stack derives the ordered commits from Git rather than a ChangePlan fixture, assigns display identities independently of U1/U2/U3, snapshots each commit with the target repository's GraphReFly runtime, compares each snapshot to its first-parent stack predecessor using upstream structural delta semantics, parses each canonical Git patch into file, hunk, old-line, and new-line rows, and serves one synchronized review payload directly from those facts. Claims and GateResult may be absent when the repository has not adopted semantic records; their absence may not block structural stack review or be presented as a passing semantic gate.

The generic repository runner accepts only the pinned published upstream capability set whose graph.blueprint() emits GraphBlueprint v2 with non-empty sibling-unique Graph-owned mountId values on every non-root subgraph. It parses and freezes artifacts with parseGraphBlueprint, verifies recorded topology hashes with verifyBlueprintHash and caller-owned hashing, produces readable diagram source only through blueprintToMermaid, and computes each equal-version parent delta only through diffGraphBlueprints. Unsupported versions, missing or duplicate mount identity, diagnostics errors, version transitions, malformed evidence, hash mismatch, and helper failure produce an explicit unavailable/error state rather than a Stack-derived fallback diagram or delta.

The strict graphrefly.stack.repository.v1 config at .graphrefly-stack.json contains only schema and blueprint.entrypoint, with a normalized repository-relative file path that may not escape the worktree. The canonical command is graphrefly-stack review --repo <path> --base <revision> --head <revision>. Its graphrefly.stack.review.v1 payload records repository label, exact target GraphReFly version, configured entrypoint, resolved base and head OIDs, and ordered ReviewCommit values containing immutable OID, parent OID, subject, hashed GraphBlueprint v2, upstream Mermaid source, upstream parent delta events, and a server-parsed structured Git diff. The base Blueprint is evidence for the first delta but is not a synthetic WorkUnit. Semantic status is explicitly not-configured when accepted records are absent, and no validity verdict is emitted. Entrypoint stdout must be exactly one JSON value within the timeout and size bound; stderr, absolute paths, environment values, and raw provider output do not enter the browser payload.

A checked-in historical run consists only of a small human index and one strict portable evidence bundle that embeds one BundleManifest and exactly the manifest-bound logical artifacts. The loader accepts the portable file directly or its containing directory, rejects schema errors, duplicate or mismatched manifest paths, unmanifested embedded values, missing values, and canonical SHA-256 mismatches, then projects review data from verified in-memory artifacts. Expanded artifact paths are an internal logical namespace, not checked-in files; ordinary exports, generated repositories, worktrees, raw provider output, local review records, comments and caches remain outside the working tree under ignored private paths or .git/grfs. Only an explicit content-addressed export may move policy-allowed comments, decisions or redacted evidence into a portable bundle for sharing or future hosted upload.

The public package is @graphrefly/stack and exposes only the grfs binary. Its packed files contain the executable, local review UI, JSON schemas, and required replay assets without monorepo-relative runtime reads or workspace dependencies. The reproducible MVP path remains a repository-local developer dependency invoked with pnpm exec grfs; a future global launcher may be optional but must prefer the repository-local engine rather than silently replacing its version. grfs init --repo <path> --graph-module <path> [--graph-export <name>] creates the strict config and a deterministic root adapter, refusing path escape or overwrite unless --force. Review snapshots the configured bounded adapter bytes and injects them only into isolated detached revisions where the entrypoint is absent, so onboarding after an existing stack does not require rewriting Git history; the referenced root Graph module remains revision-owned and must exist. grfs review resolves one installed exact @graphrefly/ts version in >=0.3.0 <0.4.0, validates required public Blueprint v2 capabilities, and requires every reviewed revision's dependencies or devDependencies declaration to accept that exact version. Recognized lockfiles must exist but may change content or type across the range. npm-pack acceptance installs the tarball into an independent repository, runs init and review through pnpm exec grfs across registry-style symlink execution and pre-onboarding commits, serves the embedded UI, and proves no workspace path is required.

The installed @graphrefly/stack package exposes the grfs binary with init, fixture create, plan, gate, replan, review, and export commands. Every command supports a versioned --json envelope on stdout while diagnostics use stderr; success exits 0, a successfully computed blocked GateResult exits 2, and usage, schema, or runtime failure exits 1. Non-JSON review binds only to 127.0.0.1 and serves the embedded read-only web projection from the same local process.

The installed @graphrefly/stack package preserves the C30 command, JSON-envelope and exit-code protocol. Non-JSON review binds only to 127.0.0.1 and serves the embedded web projection from the same local process. Generic repository review remains read-only for source files, Git objects, refs, GateResult and repository configuration, but may expose one bounded same-origin application/json endpoint that validates and appends strict immutable local review decisions below the real Git common directory at .git/grfs. Each decision binds base, head, parent and selected commit OIDs plus Blueprint hash, records an unverified local reviewer, summary and timestamp, and is never rebound after Git or Blueprint changes. GET projection and explicit download may read only records matching the current reviewed witnesses. A portable review export contains only manifest-bound, content-hashed human review artifacts and their non-source witness identifiers; comments and decisions are eligible for later hosted upload only after explicit export and repository-policy redaction, while caches, raw source, unredacted Blueprints and execution details are never included by default.
